title: Create declarative value-spec factories for dummy data overrides
blocks: Example caller wire-ups, documentation, and any downstream task that demonstrates dummy data overrides.

status_instructions.on_followup: If any factory needs a subtle semantic callers must know about (e.g. `oneOf` preserves order), append to the dummy api skeleton task's `side_context`.

description: Create `lib/generators/t_value_specs.dart` containing the declarative spec factories callers pass into `TDummyFirestoreApi`'s `fieldGenerators` / `typeGenerators` maps: `oneOf`, `oneOfEnum`, `rangeInt`, `rangeDouble`, `sentence`, `words`, `paragraph`, `pastDate`, `futureDate`, `uuid`, `fixed`. Every factory returns a `TValueGenerator` (dynamic Function()) that the registry can invoke without knowing anything about the spec internals.
context_for_junior: Callers should not have to hand-write closures like `() => ['a@acme.com', 'b@acme.com'][random.nextInt(2)]` for every override. These factory functions wrap that boilerplate behind readable names. The key gotcha: all randomness must flow through a caller-supplied `Random` so the dummy api's determinism is preserved. Since these factories are created at construction time (before the dummy api exists), they accept a Random through a thread-local mechanism OR they are written as lazy generators that accept the Random on first call. The simpler choice — and the one we will use — is that every factory takes a `Random` positional or named parameter, and the dummy api forwards its own `Random` into the registry build step, which in turn passes it into any spec factories that need it. Since the spec factories must be plain `TValueGenerator` (no Random arg), we instead adopt a pattern where the factories are created with a Random closure captured at caller-site, OR the registry wraps non-Random-aware generators with its own Random. Read the spec's `determinism` section for the exact rule before picking an approach.
why_it_matters: Callers touch these factories directly. A leaky abstraction here means every feature ends up writing its own closure soup. Determinism is non-negotiable — if a factory uses `Random()` from thin air, it breaks the seeded-run guarantee the entire feature promises.
instructions: Create `lib/generators/t_value_specs.dart` exporting the factories listed in the spec's `declarative_value_specs.factories`.

Decide the Random-injection pattern before writing any factory: the approved approach is that every factory accepts an optional `Random? random` named parameter; when null, it captures the `Random` at invocation time from a package-private injection point the registry sets up during its `build` call. If that mechanism feels fragile, switch to making factories take `Random` explicitly and document in the factory's dartdoc that callers should pass the dummy api's seed through. Pick one pattern and keep every factory consistent.

Implement each factory exactly per the spec: `oneOf<V>(List<V> values)`, `oneOfEnum<E extends Enum>(List<E> values)`, `rangeInt(int min, int max)`, `rangeDouble(double min, double max)`, `sentence({int wordCount = 8})`, `words({int min = 1, int max = 3})`, `paragraph({int sentences = 3})`, `pastDate({Duration within = const Duration(days: 365)})`, `futureDate({Duration within = const Duration(days: 365)})`, `uuid()`, `fixed<V>(V value)`.
`fixed` returns the exact supplied value every call — it does not use Random at all.
`sentence`, `words`, `paragraph` produce lowercase lorem-style text using a small internal word pool declared as a `const List<String>`. Do not pull a package dependency for lorem.

`uuid` returns a deterministic id of the shape `'dummy_' + index.toString().padLeft(8, '0')` where `index` is sourced from the shared Random's next int call — not a true UUID v4, because determinism is the contract.
Every factory returns `TValueGenerator` (not a subclass). The registry adopts them as-is.
Run `make analyze package=turbo_firestore_api` and the new tests.
goals: Every caller override listed in the spec is expressible in one line at the caller's DI wire-up.
All randomness routes through the caller-supplied Random.
No external dependencies added for text generation.
non_goals: No real UUIDs — determinism wins over RFC correctness.
No integration with the probing map.
No example usage docs in CLAUDE.md or README.
acceptance_criteria: Unit tests prove each factory returns a `TValueGenerator`.
Unit tests prove determinism: two Random(42)-seeded runs of the same factory chain produce identical outputs.
`fixed(42)` returns `42` on ten calls in a row.
`oneOf(['a','b','c'])` returns one of the supplied values on every call.
constraints: No `dart:math` instances created inside factories — the Random must be injected.
No shared mutable state other than the injected Random.
Trailing commas, single quotes, const constructors.

testing.rules: No mocks. Inject `Random(seed)` directly into every factory and assert on outcome shape. Avoid hardcoded string comparisons for text factories — assert structural properties (word count, contains a space, starts with a letter) instead.
scenarios: Given `oneOf([1,2,3])` wired to `Random(0)`, When called 100 times, Then every returned value is in {1,2,3}.
Given `rangeInt(10, 20)`, When called 100 times, Then every returned value is in [10, 20).
Given `pastDate()`, When called, Then the returned DateTime is before `DateTime.now()`.
Given two factory chains built with `Random(42)`, When each is walked in the same order, Then their outputs are element-wise equal.
Given `fixed('pinned')`, When called repeatedly, Then every call returns `'pinned'`.
testing.refactor_for_testability: If the Random injection point becomes awkward (e.g. global setter), refactor factories to take Random as a required param and update dependent tasks' side_context.

quality_bar.maintainability: Adding a new factory is one function plus one test file entry.
quality_bar.dry: No duplicated randomness plumbing — shared helpers live in one private section.
quality_bar.single_responsibility: Each factory produces exactly one value shape.
quality_bar.performance: Each invocation is O(1) plus any list-size cost for `oneOf` (O(1) with nextInt).
quality_bar.abuse_resistance: Factories that receive out-of-range args (`rangeInt(20, 10)`) throw ArgumentError at construction.
